content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 4, 2018

Sarah Lang
Attorney
Bell McAndrews & Hiltaclk, LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-18-160

Dear Ms. Lang:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the advertisement disclaimer provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating it as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

May a recipient committee satisfy the "top contributor" advertisement disclosure requirement as to affiliated entities qualifying as a top contributor by displaying the "name of filer" appearing on the entities' most recent Major Donor Report?

CONCLUSION

Yes. By disclosing the "name of filer" on an advertisement, a recipient committee provides the public with the necessary information to identify the source of the contributions supporting the advertisement and the individual or entity that directed and controlled the contributions.

BACKGROUND AND ANALYSIS

The Act sets forth advertisement disclosure requirements meant to inform the public of the source and nature of an advertisement. These requirements include "top contributor" disclosure, which requires certain recipient committees to display its "top contributors" on

____________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-18-160
Page No. 2

committee advertisements. (Section 84503(a).) This requirement was recently added to the
Act by Assembly Bill 249 (Stats. 2017, Ch. 546, AB 249 Mullin).

The Act defines "top contributors" for purposes of this disclosure as "the persons
from whom the committee paying for an advertisement has received its three highest
cumulative contributions of fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) or more." (Section
84501(c)(1).)³ The term "person" is defined as "an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability
company, association, committee, and any other organization or group of persons acting in
concert."

Your inquiry concerns how affiliated entities should be identified on an
advertisement when the entities' aggregate contributions qualify them as a top contributor.
More specifically, you seek to confirm whether a recipient committee may satisfy the "top
contributor" disclosure requirement as to such entities by displaying the "name of filer"
appearing on the entities' most recent Major Donor Report.

Name of Filer

Regulation 18428 provides that affiliated entities must identify themselves on their
Major Donor Reports by their "name of filer." The "name of filer" designation for affiliated
entities is a combination of the name of the individual, or one of the contributing entities,
that directs and controls the relevant political activity and a statement indicating that the
report includes the aggregated activities of other entities directed and controlled by the
filer.⁴

Affiliated Entities

When a person directs and controls the activity of another, both persons are
considered "affiliated entities" and treated as one person for purposes of contribution limits,
reporting, registration of committees, and gift limits under the Act. (See for example Meyn
Advice Letter, A-05-242; Fechner Advice Letter, I-03-263.)⁵ This principle derives from the
Commission Opinion, In re Lumsdon, 2 FPPC Ops. 140, which found that when an
individual directs and controls an entity, as a majority shareholder for example, the
individual and the entity should be treated as one "person" rather than separate persons.
While Lumsdon, supra, primarily focuses on the treatment of affiliated entities in the context
of committee registration, the Commission and Legislature later extended its reasoning in

³ The term "cumulative contributions" is defined as "the cumulative amount of contributions received by a
committee beginning 12 months before the date of the expenditure and ending seven days before the time the
advertisement is sent to the printer or broadcaster." (Section 84501(b).)

⁴ This requirement may be satisfied by providing the phrase, "including aggregated contributions/independent
expenditures," following the name of the person directing and controlling the political activity. (Regulation
18428(b)(1).

⁵ The determination as to whether a person directs and controls another is a fact-driven inquiry made on a case-
by-case basis. However, such direction and control is presumed in the context of political contributing under certain
circumstances. (See Section 85311.)

File No. 1-18-160
Page No. 3

regards to affiliation to various aspects of the Act, as noted above. (See Section 85311;
Kanner Advice Letter, I-05-168.)

Consistent with the intent and purposes of the Act and AB 249, as well as the
definition of "person" to include a group acting in concert under the Act, we find it
appropriate to extend this reasoning to the context of "top contributor" disclosure for
advertisements. Therefore, pursuant to that finding, affiliated entities whose aggregate
contributions trigger "top contributor" disclosure should be identified on advertisements as a
single "person."

Identification of Affiliated Entities as "Top Contributors"

The aim of "top contributor disclosure" is to "provide accurate information to
voters, especially [about] who is truly paying for campaign advertisements." (Assem. Com.
on Elections and Redistricting, analysis of Assem. Bill No. AB 249, September 14, 2017, p.
4.) Specifically, the author noted that AB 249 sought to remedy having top donors "hide
behind multiple layers of organizations to intentionally mislead advertisement viewers."
(Id.) You suggest that the disclosure of the "name of filer" associated with affiliated entities
qualifying as "top contributors" would achieve that objective and satisfy the disclosure
requirements under Section 84503.

Disclosure of the "name of filer" ensures that the person directing and controlling
contributions made by affiliated entities is disclosed on the relevant advertisement. While
the source of the contributions (i.e., name of the entity listed on the contributor check) may
in some instances be omitted from the advertisement under that approach, the public can
access such information by reviewing the affiliated entities' Major Donor Reports on the
California Secretary of State's campaign finance database, Cal-Access, or with the city or
county the statement is filed. We recognize that requiring the names of the affiliated
contributors alone would not provide the important information as to the "person" directing
and controlling the contributions; and requiring both (name of filer and names of
contributors) may be too lengthy for the forum of an advertisement.

Therefore, consistent with the purpose and intent of "top contributor" disclosure, we find
that a recipient committee may satisfy its disclosure obligations as to affiliated entities qualifying as
a "top contributor" by displaying the "name of filer" appearing on the entities' most recent Major
Donor Report.

Additionally, Section 84503(b) provides:

"(b) The disclosure of a top contributor pursuant to this section need not include
terms such as "incorporated," "committee," "political action committee," or
"corporation," or abbreviations of these terms, unless the term is part of the
contributor's name in common usage or parlance."

Therefore, to the extent that the "name of the filer" appearing on an entity's most recent
major donor report includes terms such as "incorporated," "committee," "political action

File No. 1-18-160
Page No. 4

committee," or "corporation," or abbreviations of these terms, such terms need not be included on
the advertisement unless the term is part of the contributor's name in common usage or parlance.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

David Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

requirement as to affiliated entities qualifying as a top contributor by displaying the "name of filer"

advertisement and the individual or entity that directed and controlled the contributions.

QUESTION: May a recipient committee satisfy the "top contributor" advertisement disclosure 
requirement as to affiliated entities qualifying as a top contributor by displaying the "name of filer" 
appearing on the entities' most recent Major Donor Report?

CONCLUSION: Yes. By disclosing the "name of filer" on an advertisement, a recipient committee provides 
the public with the necessary information to identify the source of the contributions supporting the 
advertisement and the individual or entity that directed and controlled the contributions.
